questions.stem: During a wound assessment, the nurse observes a pressure injury where the base is entirely covered with yellow, adherent slough and the wound depth cannot be determined. The nurse correctly documents this as:

options.text: Stage III pressure injury
Stage IV pressure injury
Unstageable pressure injury
Suspected deep tissue pressure injury

questions.rationale: An unstageable pressure injury is one in which the base is obscured by slough (yellow, tan, gray) or eschar, preventing accurate staging. The wound cannot be assessed as Stage III or IV without debriding the wound bed. Suspected deep tissue injury (D) presents as intact or non-intact skin with deep purple or maroon discoloration or blood-filled blister from underlying soft tissue damage — not covered with slough. Once debrided, the wound can be restaged.

distractorRationales.a: "Stage III pressure injury" misses the bedside risk because during a wound assessment, the nurse observes a pressure injury where the base is entirely covered with yellow points to infection progression. Infection control or sepsis escalation limits spread and organ hypoperfusion before lower-acuity care.
distractorRationales.b: The option can leave infection progression untreated because it answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. "Unstageable pressure injury" fits the safer response to the wound cannot be assessed as Stage III or IV without debriding the wound bed.
distractorRationales.d: This distractor shifts care toward misprioritization instead of infection or sepsis escalation. The stem cue "The wound cannot be assessed as Stage III or IV without debriding the wound bed" makes infection progression the active safety concern.

questions.takeaway: Unstageable = base obscured by slough/eschar, cannot determine depth. Suspected deep tissue injury = intact purple/maroon discoloration.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: Which age-related physiological changes MOST directly alter drug pharmacokinetics in elderly patients, creating the highest risk of toxicity?

options.text: Increased gastric acid production and increased GI motility → reduced oral drug absorption for most medications
Decreased renal GFR, decreased hepatic mass and blood flow, and increased body fat percentage → renally and hepatically cleared drugs accumulate and fat-soluble drugs have prolonged half-lives
Increased plasma albumin levels → less free drug available, requiring higher doses in elderly
Increased cardiac output → faster distribution, shorter half-lives, and need for higher doses

questions.rationale: Key age-related pharmacokinetic changes: (1) Renal — GFR declines ~1 mL/min/year after age 40 (Cockcroft-Gault may overestimate renal function in elderly with low muscle mass) → renally cleared drugs accumulate: digoxin, metformin, aminoglycosides, NSAIDs, methotrexate; (2) Hepatic — reduced hepatic mass, blood flow, and CYP450 activity → first-pass metabolism reduced (increased oral bioavailability), phase I reactions (oxidation, reduction) more impaired than phase II (conjugation); (3) Body composition — increased fat, decreased lean mass, decreased total body water → fat-soluble drugs (benzodiazepines, diazepam, amiodarone) have larger volume of distribution and prolonged half-lives; water-soluble drugs (lithium, digoxin) reach higher concentrations with same dose; (4) Protein — decreased albumin → increased free fraction of highly protein-bound drugs (warfarin, phenytoin). GI motility decreases (not increases) with age. Cardiac output decreases.

distractorRationales.a: The safer response addresses bleeding or anticoagulation injury; "Increased gastric acid production and increased GI motility → reduced oral drug absorption for most medications" moves care toward ambulation, intake, or discharge teaching before bleeding-risk intervention.
distractorRationales.c: The safer response addresses bleeding or anticoagulation injury; "Increased plasma albumin levels → less free drug available, requiring higher doses in elderly" moves care toward routine care before bleeding-risk intervention.
distractorRationales.d: "Increased cardiac output → faster distribution, shorter half-lives, and need for higher doses" misses the bedside risk because (2) Hepatic — reduced hepatic mass, blood flow points to bleeding or anticoagulation injury. Bleeding risk requires assessment and source control before comfort measures, teaching, or routine medication timing.

questions.takeaway: Elderly pharmacokinetics: decreased GFR (drug accumulation), decreased hepatic clearance, increased fat (prolonged half-lives of fat-soluble drugs), decreased albumin (more free drug). Principle: 'Start low, go slow.'
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: An RN is concerned that a patient's deteriorating condition is not being addressed. She has called the resident twice and the attending once, but no orders have been given and the patient's condition is worsening. What is the next appropriate action?

options.text: Document that the physician was notified and continue monitoring
Initiate the chain of command by notifying the charge nurse, nursing supervisor, and if necessary the chief of staff or patient safety officer
Call the patient's family to inform them of the situation and have them contact the physician
Administer PRN medications independently based on nursing judgment

questions.rationale: When a nurse cannot get a timely response from the physician and patient safety is at risk, the chain of command must be activated: charge nurse → nursing supervisor → department director → chief of staff/chief of medicine → risk management/patient safety officer. Many hospitals also have rapid response teams (RRT) that can be activated by nursing. Documentation alone (A) does not protect the patient. Contacting family (C) violates HIPAA and does not address the immediate clinical problem. Administering medications without orders (D) is outside RN scope of practice and illegal.

distractorRationales.a: Because no orders have been given and the patient's condition is worsening suggests medication toxicity, "Document that the physician was notified and continue monitoring" would not correct the active problem. Medication safety depends on checking contraindications, labs, and adverse effects before giving or continuing the drug.
distractorRationales.c: "Call the patient's family to inform them of the situation and have them contact the physician" relies on notification without the bedside safety step. The cue "no orders have been given and the patient's condition is worsening" raises concern for medication toxicity, so medication-safety intervention is needed before waiting for a callback.
distractorRationales.d: "Administer PRN medications independently based on nursing judgment" misses the bedside risk because no orders have been given and the patient's condition is worsening points to medication toxicity. Medication safety depends on checking contraindications, labs, and adverse effects before giving or continuing the drug.

questions.takeaway: When physician is unresponsive to clinical concerns: activate chain of command (charge nurse → supervisor → administrator) and/or call rapid response — document all attempts.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: A patient with severe preeclampsia is receiving IV magnesium sulfate. The nurse assesses: RR 10, deep tendon reflexes absent, urine output 20 mL/hr, serum magnesium level 12 mEq/L. Which action is the immediate priority?

options.text: Decrease the magnesium infusion rate by 50% and continue monitoring
Stop the magnesium infusion and administer calcium gluconate 1 g IV slowly
Notify the provider and document the findings
Increase IV fluid rate to improve urine output and enhance magnesium excretion

questions.rationale: Signs of magnesium toxicity: RR <12/min, absent DTRs, urine output <25 mL/hr, serum Mg >9–10 mEq/L. This patient has multiple toxicity signs (RR 10, absent DTRs, oliguria, Mg 12 mEq/L). The infusion must be stopped immediately. Calcium gluconate 1 g IV (10 mL of 10% solution over 3–5 minutes) is the antidote — calcium antagonizes magnesium's effects. Decreasing the rate (A) is insufficient when toxicity is already established with respiratory compromise. Notifying the provider (C) happens simultaneously but stopping the infusion is the first action. Fluids (D) may help excretion but not fast enough to address impending respiratory arrest.

distractorRationales.a: "Decrease the magnesium infusion rate by 50% and continue monitoring" treats active deterioration as something safe to watch. The cue "Magnesium toxicity: stop infusion immediately + calcium gluconate 1 g IV as antidote" raises concern for oxygenation or ventilation, so airway and breathing intervention is needed before routine observation.
distractorRationales.c: This distractor shifts care toward incomplete escalation instead of airway and breathing intervention. The stem cue "Signs of magnesium toxicity: RR <12/min, absent DTRs, urine output <25 mL/hr, serum Mg >9–10 mEq/L" makes oxygenation or ventilation the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.d: Because a patient with severe preeclampsia is receiving IV magnesium sulfate suggests oxygenation or ventilation, "Increase IV fluid rate to improve urine output and enhance magnesium excretion" would not correct the active problem. Airway assessment, positioning, oxygen support, or rapid escalation protects ventilation before teaching or documentation.

questions.takeaway: Magnesium toxicity: stop infusion immediately + calcium gluconate 1 g IV as antidote; monitor RR and DTRs every hour during infusion.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: The nurse is assessing two patients. Patient A has BP 80/50, sodium 124 mEq/L, potassium 6.1 mEq/L, and hyperpigmentation. Patient B has BP 158/94, sodium 148 mEq/L, potassium 3.1 mEq/L, and truncal obesity with purple striae. Which conditions CORRECTLY match these presentations?

options.text: Patient A: Cushing's syndrome; Patient B: Addison's disease
Patient A: Addison's disease (adrenal insufficiency); Patient B: Cushing's syndrome
Patient A: Hypothyroidism; Patient B: Hyperaldosteronism
Patient A: SIADH; Patient B: Diabetes insipidus

questions.rationale: Addison's disease (primary adrenal insufficiency): cortisol and aldosterone deficiency → hyponatremia, hyperkalemia, hypotension, and hyperpigmentation (elevated ACTH stimulates melanocytes). Cushing's syndrome (cortisol excess): hypertension, hypokalemia, hypernatremia, central obesity, purple striae, and moon face from chronic cortisol excess. These are opposite presentations driven by opposite hormonal states.

distractorRationales.a: Because patient A has BP 80/50, sodium 124 mEq/L, potassium 6 suggests renal injury, "Patient A: Cushing's syndrome; Patient B: Addison's disease" would not correct the active problem. Renal protection depends on hydration, medication review, and creatinine follow-up before assuming the study is low risk.
distractorRationales.c: "Patient A: Hypothyroidism; Patient B: Hyperaldosteronism" answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. The cue "Patient A has BP 80/50, sodium 124 mEq/L, potassium 6" raises concern for renal injury, so renal protection is needed before routine care.
distractorRationales.d: The option can leave renal injury untreated because it answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. "Patient A: Addison's disease (adrenal insufficiency); Patient B: Cushing's syndrome" fits the safer response to cushing's = hyperNa, hypoK, hypertension, truncal obesity, striae.

questions.takeaway: Addison's = hypoNa, hyperK, hypotension, hyperpigmentation. Cushing's = hyperNa, hypoK, hypertension, truncal obesity, striae.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: A patient on clozapine for treatment-resistant schizophrenia develops fever 39.8°C, diaphoresis, severe muscle rigidity ('lead pipe'), altered mental status, and autonomic instability (fluctuating BP, tachycardia). Which condition is this, and what is the immediate action?

options.text: Serotonin syndrome; administer cyproheptadine and stop all serotonergic agents
Neuroleptic malignant syndrome (NMS); discontinue antipsychotic immediately and initiate supportive care with dantrolene or bromocriptine
Acute dystonia; administer IV diphenhydramine 50 mg
Anticholinergic toxicity from clozapine; administer physostigmine

questions.rationale: Neuroleptic malignant syndrome (NMS) is a life-threatening idiosyncratic reaction to antipsychotics characterized by the tetrad: hyperthermia, severe muscle rigidity, altered mental status, and autonomic instability. It develops over days-weeks. Immediate management: stop the antipsychotic, aggressive cooling, IV fluids, dantrolene (muscle relaxant) and/or bromocriptine (dopamine agonist). Serotonin syndrome (A) has similar features but presents with hyperreflexia and clonus (not lead-pipe rigidity) and requires cyproheptadine. Acute dystonia (C) does not include fever or autonomic instability.

distractorRationales.a: This distractor shifts care toward misprioritization instead of infection or sepsis escalation. The stem cue "A patient on clozapine for treatment-resistant schizophrenia develops fever 39" makes infection progression the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.c: Because serotonin syndrome (A) has similar features but presents with hyperreflexia and clonus (not lead-pipe rigidity) and requires cyproheptadine suggests infection progression, "Acute dystonia; administer IV diphenhydramine 50 mg" would not correct the active problem. Infection control or sepsis escalation limits spread and organ hypoperfusion before lower-acuity care.
distractorRationales.d: The option can leave infection progression untreated because it commits to a competing cause before checking the dangerous pattern. "Neuroleptic malignant syndrome (NMS); discontinue antipsychotic immediately and initiate supportive care with dantrolene or bromocriptine" fits the safer response to a patient on clozapine for treatment-resistant schizophrenia develops fever 39.

questions.takeaway: NMS tetrad: hyperthermia + lead-pipe rigidity + altered mental status + autonomic instability — stop antipsychotic immediately; treat with dantrolene and/or bromocriptine.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: A nurse is monitoring a patient on telemetry when the rhythm suddenly changes to a wide-complex, regular tachycardia at 180 bpm. The patient is alert, with BP 102/60 mmHg and reports palpitations but no chest pain. Which intervention should the nurse anticipate?

options.text: Immediate unsynchronized defibrillation at 200J
Prepare for synchronized cardioversion at 100J after sedation
Administer IV adenosine 6 mg rapid push to terminate the rhythm
Apply a 12-lead ECG, notify the provider, and prepare antiarrhythmic therapy (amiodarone or procainamide) while monitoring hemodynamics closely

questions.rationale: Wide-complex tachycardia (WCT) in a hemodynamically stable patient (alert, BP 102/60, no chest pain) requires accurate rhythm identification before treatment. WCT is most commonly ventricular tachycardia (VT) but can also be supraventricular tachycardia with aberrant conduction (SVT-A). A 12-lead ECG helps differentiate. If VT is confirmed: amiodarone or procainamide IV is the antiarrhythmic of choice for stable VT. Synchronized cardioversion is reserved for hemodynamically unstable stable VT (BP < 90, ischemia, altered mental status). Unsynchronized defibrillation (defib) is for pulseless VT/VF. Adenosine is appropriate for narrow-complex SVT; it is not first-line for wide-complex tachycardia and can be harmful in VT.

distractorRationales.a: The safer response addresses circulation or perfusion; "Immediate unsynchronized defibrillation at 200J" moves care toward routine care before circulation and perfusion support.
distractorRationales.b: Because a nurse is monitoring a patient on telemetry when the rhythm suddenly changes to a wide-complex, regular tachycardia suggests circulation or perfusion, "Prepare for synchronized cardioversion at 100J after sedation" would not correct the active problem. Perfusion problems require rapid assessment and stabilization before comfort measures or delayed provider follow-up.
distractorRationales.c: "Administer IV adenosine 6 mg rapid push to terminate the rhythm" misses the bedside risk because a nurse is monitoring a patient on telemetry when the rhythm suddenly changes to a wide-complex, regular tachycardia points to circulation or perfusion. Perfusion problems require rapid assessment and stabilization before comfort measures or delayed provider follow-up.

questions.takeaway: Stable wide-complex tachycardia: 12-lead first → provider → antiarrhythmic (amiodarone or procainamide for VT). Unstable VT = synchronized cardioversion. Pulseless VT = unsynchronized defibrillation. Never defibrillate a patient who has a pulse.

visualRationale.title: Cardiovascular / Dysrhythmias
visualRationale.caption: Stable wide-complex tachycardia: 12-lead first → provider → antiarrhythmic (amiodarone or procainamide for VT). Unstable VT = synchronized cardioversion. Pulseless VT = unsynchronized defibrillation. Never defibrillate a patient who has a pulse.
visualRationale.conclusion: Stable wide-complex tachycardia: 12-lead first → provider → antiarrhythmic (amiodarone or procainamide for VT). Unstable VT = synchronized cardioversion. Pulseless VT = unsynchronized defibrillation. Never defibrillate a patient who has a pulse.

questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: Parents bring their 9-month-old to the ED with episodic, inconsolable crying every 15–20 minutes, pulling the knees to the chest, and passage of stools described as 'jelly-like and dark red.' The abdomen is mildly distended. What is the priority nursing action?

options.text: Administer oral rehydration solution and observe for 2 hours
Prepare the family for a possible emergent surgical laparotomy
Notify the physician; these findings are consistent with intussusception — prepare for diagnostic and potentially therapeutic air or contrast enema
Obtain stool culture for Campylobacter and Salmonella

questions.rationale: Classic intussusception triad: episodic colicky pain (bowel telescoping and releasing), vomiting, and currant jelly stools (blood and mucus from mucosal ischemia). Most common site: ileocecal junction. Peak age: 6–36 months. Diagnosis: ultrasound (target sign). Treatment: air or hydrostatic (contrast) enema under fluoroscopy or ultrasound — diagnostic and therapeutic in ~80% of cases. Surgical reduction is required if enema fails or peritonitis present. Stool culture addresses infectious causes, not mechanical obstruction. Waiting worsens intestinal ischemia.

distractorRationales.a: The option can leave renal injury untreated because it treats active deterioration as something safe to watch. "Notify the physician; these findings are consistent with intussusception — prepare for diagnostic and potentially therapeutic" fits the safer response to intussusception: episodic colicky pain + vomiting + currant jelly stools in infant/toddler → ultrasound diagnosis.
distractorRationales.b: The safer response addresses renal injury; "Prepare the family for a possible emergent surgical laparotomy" moves care toward routine care before renal protection.
distractorRationales.d: The safer response addresses renal injury; "Obtain stool culture for Campylobacter and Salmonella" moves care toward routine care before renal protection.

questions.takeaway: Intussusception: episodic colicky pain + vomiting + currant jelly stools in infant/toddler → ultrasound diagnosis; air/contrast enema is therapeutic in 80%; surgical if enema fails.

visualRationale.title: Pediatric Nursing
visualRationale.caption: Intussusception: episodic colicky pain + vomiting + currant jelly stools in infant/toddler → ultrasound diagnosis; air/contrast enema is therapeutic in 80%; surgical if enema fails.
visualRationale.conclusion: Intussusception: episodic colicky pain + vomiting + currant jelly stools in infant/toddler → ultrasound diagnosis; air/contrast enema is therapeutic in 80%; surgical if enema fails.

questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: A morbidly obese patient with symptomatic GERD requires urgent surgery. The anesthesiologist plans rapid sequence induction (RSI). During RSI, which nursing action is most important to reduce pulmonary aspiration risk?

options.text: Place the patient in Trendelenburg position to maximize airway visualization
Apply firm cricoid pressure (Sellick maneuver) as directed by the anesthesia provider until ETT placement is confirmed
Insert a nasogastric tube immediately before induction to fully decompress the stomach
Administer metoclopramide IV push 10 minutes before induction to empty the stomach

questions.rationale: RSI is used for patients at elevated aspiration risk (obesity, GERD, full stomach, pregnancy, bowel obstruction). It combines: pre-oxygenation → simultaneous administration of a rapid induction agent (propofol/ketamine) and a fast-onset neuromuscular blocking agent (succinylcholine or high-dose rocuronium) → immediate intubation without manual ventilation. The nurse's critical role: apply firm downward pressure on the cricoid cartilage (Sellick maneuver) to compress the esophagus between the cricoid ring and the cervical vertebra, preventing passive regurgitation of gastric contents until ETT cuff is inflated and placement confirmed by capnography. If active vomiting occurs, cricoid pressure must be released immediately (holds vomiting in and increases aspiration risk). NGT cannot adequately empty the stomach in time and does not protect the airway. Trendelenburg (head-down) increases aspiration risk. Metoclopramide is adjunctive preparation, not a substitute for RSI or cricoid pressure.

distractorRationales.a: "Place the patient in Trendelenburg position to maximize airway visualization" answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. The cue "During RSI, which nursing action is most important to reduce pulmonary aspiration risk" raises concern for oxygenation or ventilation, so airway and breathing intervention is needed before routine care.
distractorRationales.c: Because if active vomiting occurs, cricoid pressure must be released immediately (holds vomiting in and increases aspiration risk) suggests oxygenation or ventilation, "Insert a nasogastric tube immediately before induction to fully decompress the stomach" would not correct the active problem. Airway assessment, positioning, oxygen support, or rapid escalation protects ventilation before teaching or documentation.
distractorRationales.d: The option can leave oxygenation or ventilation untreated because it answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. "Apply firm cricoid pressure (Sellick maneuver) as directed by the anesthesia provider until ETT placement is" fits the safer response to trendelenburg (head-down) increases aspiration risk.

questions.takeaway: RSI aspiration prevention: nurse applies cricoid pressure (Sellick maneuver) — compresses esophagus to prevent passive regurgitation during intubation. Release if active vomiting occurs. Used for all high-risk aspiration patients.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.

questions.stem: A nurse on a step-down unit is monitoring four patients. Which clinical finding requires the nurse to call the rapid response team (RRT) IMMEDIATELY?

options.text: A 60-year-old with pneumonia whose respiratory rate has increased from 18 to 26 breaths/min over the last 2 hours and is now using accessory muscles
A 74-year-old with atrial fibrillation with a ventricular rate of 88 bpm, unchanged from admission
A 55-year-old post-cholecystectomy who reports nausea and has vomited once
A 66-year-old with CHF whose morning weight is 2 lbs higher than yesterday

questions.rationale: A rising respiratory rate to 26/min with accessory muscle use in a pneumonia patient signals deteriorating respiratory status and impending respiratory failure—a classic RRT trigger. Tachypnea > 25/min, increased work of breathing, and a worsening trend over 2 hours meets standard early warning score thresholds for team activation. The AFib patient is at a controlled, unchanged rate (not a new emergency). Post-op nausea with one emesis is common and manageable on the unit. A 2-lb overnight weight gain in CHF warrants provider notification but not RRT activation unless accompanied by respiratory distress.

distractorRationales.b: "A 74-year-old with atrial fibrillation with a ventricular rate of 88 bpm, unchanged from admission" misses the bedside risk because a 2-lb overnight weight gain in CHF warrants provider notification but not RRT activation unless accompanied by respiratory points to oxygenation or ventilation. Airway assessment, positioning, oxygen support, or rapid escalation protects ventilation before teaching or documentation.
distractorRationales.c: "A 55-year-old post-cholecystectomy who reports nausea and has vomited once" misses the bedside risk because a 2-lb overnight weight gain in CHF warrants provider notification but not RRT activation unless accompanied by respiratory points to oxygenation or ventilation. Airway assessment, positioning, oxygen support, or rapid escalation protects ventilation before teaching or documentation.
distractorRationales.d: This distractor shifts care toward premature attribution instead of airway and breathing intervention. The stem cue "a worsening trend over 2 hours meets standard early warning score thresholds for team activation" makes oxygenation or ventilation the active safety concern.

questions.takeaway: RRT triggers include: RR > 25, acute accessory muscle use, O2 sat drop, acute altered mental status, SBP < 90. Trends matter as much as absolute values—worsening over 2 hours is a red flag.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the NCLEX review-batch library with recent-family avoidance.
